steps.text: Verify if only undried wood particulates (green materials) are collected and conveyed, and whether the equipment handling or storing the material is entirely non-combustible.
Conduct dust sample testing to determine the percentage of combustible dust present in the total dust cloud or pile.

Apply 29 CFR 1910.22 for housekeeping on walking-working surfaces, except in grain handling facilities, explosive and blasting agent operations, coal-fired electrical power generating stations, and storage areas.
Apply 29 CFR 1910.176 paragraph (c) for housekeeping in storage areas.
Use 29 CFR 1910.272 for inspecting grain-handling facilities.
Refer to the Inspection of Grain Handling Facilities Directive (CPL 02-01-004) for guidance when performing combustible dust inspections for grain handling facilities.
Evaluate the specific conditions of the hazard and the objectives of protection to choose the most effective and reliable means for explosion control.
Provide deflagration venting to a safe area in accordance with NFPA 68.
Protect the equipment from the effects of a deflagration by deflagration venting, deflagration suppression, or deflagration pressure containment.
Ask questions early in the inspection.

Review process-related documents (including P&IDs, process flow diagrams, process narratives, equipment description manuals, operating procedures, manufacturer documentation, and maintenance records) as part of the equipment inspections.

Apply the following standards to the design and operation of the new Port Wentworth facility: NFPA 61: Standard for the Prevention of Fires and Dust Explosions in Agricultural and Food Processing Facilities; NFPA 499: Recommended Practice for the Classification of Combustible Dusts and Hazardous (Classified) Locations for Electrical Installations in Chemical Process Areas; NFPA 654: Standard for the Prevention of Fire and Dust Explosions from the Manufacturing, Processing, and Handling of Combustible Particulate Solids; NFPA Handbook, Electrical Installations in Hazardous Locations; NFPA 70 Article 500: Hazardous (Classified) Locations.
Conduct a comprehensive review of all existing Imperial Sugar Company sugar manufacturing facilities against the standards listed in recommendations and implement identified corrective actions.

Implement a corporate-wide comprehensive housekeeping program to control combustible dust accumulation that will ensure sugar dust, cornstarch dust, or other combustible dust does not accumulate to hazardous quantities on overhead horizontal surfaces, packing equipment, and floors.
Develop training materials that address combustible dust hazards and train all employees and contractors at all Imperial Sugar Company facilities.
Require periodic (e.g., annual) refresher training for all employees and contractors.

Improve the emergency evacuation policies and procedures at the Port Wentworth facility; specifically: install an emergency alert (alarm) system in the facility, and require routine emergency evacuation drills and critiques.
Apply chapters 12, 13, 15, and 16 of NFPA 484-2012 for all new and existing equipment and operations processing combustible dusts or powders.
Develop training materials that address combustible dust hazards and plant-specific metal dust hazards.
Train all employees and contractors on combustible dust hazards and plant-specific metal dust hazards.
Conduct a process hazard analysis as defined in NFPA 484-2012, Section 12.2.5.
Submit a copy of the process hazard analysis to the local fire department or the enforcing authority for the fire code.
Measure, obtain/review the manual, contact the manufacturer, and review applicable NFPA standards to determine the total interior volume, dirty side volume, and exemption status of the AMS.
Review AMS design and field installation to determine how the filtered material leaves the bottom of the AMS and the connection between the material hopper and the discharge container.

Review AMS design and field installation, identify markings, contact the manufacturer, and review applicable NFPA standards to obtain rotary valve details and determine if it is rated as a deflagration propagation protection device.
Review AMS design and field installation to determine where the filtered material discharges, the container material (if applicable), or how it is transported (if no container).
Review AMS design and field installation to determine the location of the fan package relative to the AMS.
Ask questions and review AMS design and field installation to determine where the filtered air discharges.
Exercise caution in determining whether a published MIE adequately represents the actual MIE of a given dust.
Obtain additional information regarding combustible dust MIEs prior to sampling.
Evaluate if the dust MIE is ≤ 30 mJ (or likely to be) or if the dust is an extremely fine powder.
Obtain samples necessary to support an enforcement action in a safe manner and in coordination with supervisor.
Suspend the 200 mesh (75µm) screen sample in a Hartmann stainless steel 1.2 liter explosion chamber and ignite with an electrical spark.
Record the maximum pressure and rate of pressure rise developed by the explosion.
Calculate ES as the product of the maximum explosion pressure (Pmax) and the maximum rate of pressure rise (Kst), normalized to Pittsburgh coal dust.
Test the sample in the 20-liter explosibility testing chamber to determine if the sample is explosive.
Mark out an area to be sampled, measure and record this area in field notes.
Take depth measurements in the area to get an average depth of the area to be sampled and record in field notes.
Collect all the material in the marked area in a 1-liter sample bottle.
Send the sample bottle, along with the depth and area measurements, to the lab.
Dry the sample for 24 hours in a drying oven set at 167°F or 75°C.
Weigh the sample.
Determine the bulk density based on the volume and weight of the sample.
Determine the maximum pressure in a contained deflagration (Pmax) and the deflagration index (KSt) of the particulate representative within the vessel.
Design the deflagration suppression system based on the worst case scenario properties of the particulate representative within the vessel at the facility's process.
Install the deflagration suppression system.
Perform servicing and maintenance on the deflagration suppression system or on vessels protected by these systems.

preconditions.text: Grain-handling facilities are located at the site.
A building or building compartment contains a dust explosion hazard outside of equipment.
An explosion hazard exists within any operating equipment greater than 8 ft3 (0.2 m3) of containing volume.
A rotary valve is utilized at the material hopper discharge opening
There is a question regarding whether a published MIE adequately represents the actual MIE.
MIE of a material is ≤ 30 mJ (or likely to be) OR dust is observed to be an extremely fine powder.
If no explosion occurs in the Hartmann Class II chamber
After receiving the sample at the SLTC lab

constraints.text: Moisture content cannot be considered an effective explosion preventive safeguard since most ignition sources provide more than enough energy to vaporize ambient moisture in the air and to ignite the dust.
Fugitive emissions from green material processes or equipment can settle on elevated surfaces and eventually dry out (to less than 25 percent moisture), presenting fire and deflagration hazards.
Dry wood particles can mix with green wood particles, resulting in a mixture of less than 25 percent moisture.
Combustible dust from grain not in a grain handling facility is not covered by 29 CFR 1910.272.
Venting of deflagrations only reduces the damage that results from combustion.
Do not use NFPA 68 venting for detonations, bulk auto-ignition, pressure from external fire, runaway exothermic reactions, or oxygen-enriched atmospheres (unless supported by test data).
NFPA 85 should not be used to address combustible dust hazards at coal-fired power plants. See 29 CFR 1910.269 as the vertical standard applicable at coal-fired power plants.
Prohibit the use of sprinkler systems and water deluge systems in all buildings that process or store combustible metals.
Wear PPE described in the NEP
Dangerous-when-wet dusts react with water/moisture to release hydrogen gas. These dusts do not have to be dispersed in air to ignite.
In cases of hybrid mixtures, other hazards, or where MIE is excessively low (≤ 1 mJ), do not collect a sample without AO and RO approval.
Fire and explosion hazard in Mills (size reduction)
Frictional heating and/or sparking from introduction of foreign materials (e.g., tramp metal or stones)
Frictional heating and/or sparking from mechanical components (e.g., internal components or bearings rubbing or striking the mill housing due to misalignment / mechanical failure)
Frictional heating of internal material due to improper feed rates, plugging, or material degradation
Electrostatic build-up/discharge
Potential dust cloud formation from grinding
Fire and/or explosion hazard in Mixers (blending)
Frictional heating and/or sparking from mechanical components or bearings (e.g., internal components rubbing or striking) in Mixers
Potential ignition source: Electrostatic build-up/discharge
Potential ignition source: Frictional heating and/or sparking from fan/blower impeller misalignment
Potential ignition source: Overheating of internal material from improper operation
Control consideration: Bonding/grounding
Control consideration: Deflagration venting, deflagration suppression, pressure containment, and inerting
Fire and/or explosion hazard associated with dust collector operations.
Potential ignition source: Upstream operations generating heat, sparks, embers, or fire.
Potential ignition source: Electrostatic build-up/discharge via any isolated conductors (e.g., metal filter clamps, collars, cages, support frames, or wires).
Potential ignition source: Filter failure and subsequent introduction of particulate into fan.
Potential ignition source: Sparking from introduction of foreign materials (e.g., tramp metal, or stones).
Potential dust cloud formation mechanism: Filter cleaning suspends dust and often forms dense clouds.

Potential control considerations: Bonding/grounding, fire detection and suppression/abort systems, routine maintenance (e.g., to fans/blowers, filter replacement), deflagration venting, deflagration suppression, deflagration isolation systems, and location.
Design, install, and maintain the system using the manufacturer's recommendations and NFPA guidance.
Servicing and maintenance requires hazardous energy control and/or isolation depending on the hazard scenario (e.g., 1910.147).
